title: NA-ULIP
word_text:
https://www.bajajallianzlife.com/life-insurance-guide/life/types-of-life-insurance-policies-in-india.html
Types of life insurance
Life insurance plans help in financial planning by creating a secure corpus on the unfortunate death of the life assured during the policy term. With a life insurance policy, you can get financial security for yourself and your family.
Moreover, there are different types of life insurance plans with their own set of benefits and relevance. You can choose from these policies based on your financial goals, investment horizon and needs. So, let’s assess the different types of life insurance plans available in the market and what each plan means.
8 Popular Types of Life Insurance Plans
As mentioned earlier, there are different types of life insurance plans available in the market. Let’s look at the eight most popular types of plans –
Term Insurance Plans:As the name suggests, term insurance is purchased for a specific tenure. If the insured dies during the plan's tenure, the nominee of the insured receives a death benefit.Check out the various features of the plan:
A term plan is purchased for a fixed tenure.
Term insurance is a life cover offering death benefits if the insured dies during the tenure.
There are various types of term insurance plans, such as level cover, increasing cover, decreasing cover and term plan with a return of premium.
The plan may give you a choice to add riders on payment of an additional nominal premium, such as:
Accidental Permanent Total and Partial Disability Rider
Waiver of Premium Rider
Accidental Death Benefit Rider
Critical Illness Benefit Rider, among others.
A term insurance calculator helps you calculate the coverage you may need for your family in your absence.
Pure term insurance plans do not offer any maturity benefit. TROP (Term Plan with Return of Premium) has a maturity benefit wherein the premium paid for the plan is returned to the policyholder if he survives the policy tenure, subject to certain deductions.
Tax Benefit:
The premium paid towards a term insurance plan is deductible under Section 80C (under the Old Tax Regime) of the Income Tax Act, 1961, up to Rs 1.5 lakhs per annum, subject to the provisions stated therein.
Maturity benefit, in the case of term plans with return of premium, is also tax-free under section 10(10D), subject to satisfaction of conditions mentioned therein.
As per section 10(10D) of the Income Tax Act, 1961, the death benefits received by your nominee under the plan are tax-free.
ULIPs – Unit Linked Insurance Plans:ULIP stands for Unit Linked Insurance Plans. If you are looking for a comprehensive insurance plan that offers both life cover and a market-linked investment component, ULIP can be a suitable choice. Through ULIPs, the premium you pay is invested in market-linked funds, post deduction of applicable charges. Along with market-linked returns, ULIPs also provide a life cover to the life assured. It is essential to note that ULIP is a type of life insurance suitable for those who want a long-term investment plan, as it comes with a 5-year lock-in period.Let's have a look at some of the features of ULIPs:
ULIP provides both a life cover component and a market-linked investment component. The policyholder can choose from various market-linked funds as per their requirements.
Money can be invested in different funds like equity funds, debt funds, hybrid funds or balanced funds as per your risk appetite. Over time, you may switch your funds under ULIP depending on the terms and conditions of the plan.
You can use the ULIP calculator that uses data provided by you to give an idea of the estimated returns. The expected rate of return, premium amount and duration can be modified to get different results as required.
Tax Benefit:
ULIPs offer three types of tax benefits:
The premium is deductible under section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961(under the old tax regime), up to Rs 1.5 lakhs a year
For ULIPs issued on or after February 1, 2021, the maturity amount remains tax-free if the aggregate annual premium in any financial year during tenure of the policy/policies does not exceed Rs 2.5 lakh and subject to satisfaction of Section 10(10D) conditions.
For ULIPs issued before February 01, 2021, the maturity benefits are exempt from tax, subject to the satisfaction of conditions mentioned in Section 10(10D) of the Income Tax Act, 1961
However, for ULIPs issued on or after February 01, 2021, with an annual premium of more than Rs. 2.50 lakhs, gains will be taxable as Capital gains. The LTCG will be tax-free up to Rs. .1.25 lakh for the given financial year for all ULIPs.
Moreover, LTCG gains beyond the exempted limits will be taxable @ 12.5% for all types of ULIPs, whether equity or debt-oriented.
The death benefit is tax-exempt under section 10(10D) of the Income Tax Act 1961, irrespective of the date of issuance of the ULIP policy.
ULIPs give you the option to switch the funds you invest in during the policy term, subject to policy terms & conditions. These switches are not taxable.
Endowment Insurance Plans:Some people want a life cover that can also offer a savings option. Under an endowment plan, you get both of these options. However, if the insured passes away during the tenure, the nominee receives the sum assured and the savings components & the periodic bonuses, if any, depending on the Insurer and policy terms and conditions. This way, an endowment plan ensures both life cover and savings benefits. Take a quick look at the various features of an endowment plan:
The premium paid for the endowment plan is used to provide life insurance cover to the insured and also offer a savings component.
Tax Benefits:
The maturity benefit is tax-free under section 10(10D) of the Income Tax Act, 1961, subject to the satisfaction of conditions mentioned therein.
The policyholder can claim deductions on the premiums paid under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961, up to Rs 1.5 lakhs a year, as per the provisions stated therein under the Old Tax Regime.
Whole Life Insurance Plans:A whole life insurance plan, as the name suggests, is an insurance plan that provides cover for your whole life, i.e up to the age of 99 or 100 years, depending upon the insurer. This is a type of life insurance plan that has both survival benefits and death benefits. A whole life insurance plan provides a death benefit to your nominees after you are no longer around. Here is a quick look at some of the features of the whole life insurance plan:
A whole life insurance plan provides coverage to the life assured until he/she turn 99 or 100 years old.
Upon the insured's death during the plan's tenure, the nominee receives a death benefit.
The plan may also pay out a survival benefit if the life assured survives the policy term, depending on the terms and conditions of the policy.
Tax Benefits:
The premiums paid under the policy are deductible up to Rs 1.5 lakh under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961, subject to provisions stated therein under the Old Tax Regime.
The death benefit as well as the maturity benefit of a whole life insurance plan are exempted from tax under Section 10 (10D) of the Income Tax Act, 1961, subject to satisfaction of the provisions stated therein.
Child Insurance Plans:Once a child is born, parents experience a lot of responsibilities. To fulfil a number of these, parents may require adequate finances. Some new parents may start investing for their children as soon as they are born. Child insurance plans are one such investment. These plans provide a combination of protection and wealth creation to ensure a secure financial future for your child. A child insurance plan helps build a steady corpus that can be used to finance your child’s life goals. The life cover that comes along with it offers a death benefit to your nominee(s) if something happens to you.A child insurance plan provides payouts at different milestones of your child’s life. It may be for their higher education, wedding, etc.Some of the features of the plan are:
A child insurance plan provides a maturity benefit to the child and a life insurance cover to the insured parent
Some child insurance plans have a waiver of premium, wherein if the insured parent dies within the policy tenure, the future premiums are waived and the plan continues as per the original schedule.
The child/nominee receives a death benefit if the insured passes away during the tenure. However, the plan continues to pay the survival/maturity benefit of the plan as per the plan’s terms & conditions.
Group Insurance Plans:As the name suggests, a group insurance plan is meant for a group of people. Under a single life insurance policy, a group of people are covered. Under an employer-employee group life insurance scheme, an organisation purchases a group life insurance plan to provide its employees’ life insurance coverage. In the case of a company, the employer takes out a group life insurance policy to offer life insurance coverage to its employees, and the employer pays the premium for the same. However, in some cases, a part of the premium is shared between the employee and the employer as well. An employee is covered under a group life policy only while they are employed with the company.Some of the features of Group Insurance Plans under the Employer-Employee Scheme:
A group life insurance covers a whole group under a single life insurance plan.
The employer is the master policyholder and typically pays the premium on behalf of its employees
Providing group life insurance can give a sense of financial security to the employees
Tax Benefits:
If an employee contributes towards the premium for the plan, they can claim the premiums paid as deductions under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961 (under the Old Tax Regime)
The death benefit payout is also completely exempt from taxation under section 10(10D) of the Income Tax Act, 1961. subject to the provisions mentioned therein.
Money Back Insurance Plans:Money Back Insurance Plans provide life insurance with savings. You can get life coverage while getting periodic payments during the policy period. It is a suitable product for someone wanting insurance protection and a guaranteed* income. Here's a detailed breakdown of their features and benefits:
These plans offer regular payouts throughout the policy term. These payments are a fixed percentage of the sum assured and help meet big expenses (like children's education, the cost of maintaining a home, and medical expenses, etc).
If the life assured dies during the policy term, the nominee (or beneficiaries) receives the death benefit.
If the life assured survives the policy term, they receive the sum assured plus any bonuses accrued during the policy duration. This creates savings for the policyholder upon the policy's maturity.
Money-back insurance Plans are available for different tenures, such as 10 years, 15 years, or 20 years, so you can find the plan that is right for you and your needs. There is flexibility with respect to policy duration and the option for a joint policy or rider.
Tax Benefits:
Premiums paid for Money Back Insurance Plans are eligible for tax deductions under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act (under the Old tax regime). You can claim a deduction of up to ₹1.5 lakh annually on premiums if you opt for the old tax regime.1
The sum assured received at maturity, including bonuses, is tax-free under Section 10(10D), provided the premiums do not exceed 10% of the sum assured.1
Annuity plans:Annuity plans are retirement-oriented life insurance plans that help you secure your golden years financially. There are two types of annuity plans, deferred and immediate annuity, and you can choose a plan that matches your needs. Some of the salient features of annuity plans are as follows –
Deferred annuity plans are savings-oriented plans that allow you to create a retirement corpus over your active working life.
Deferred annuity plans can be offered as traditional endowment plans or ULIPs
Immediate annuity plans are meant to create a regular and steady inflow of annuity in your golden years.
You have to pay a single premium, called the purchase price, to buy immediate annuity plans.
Under deferred annuity plans, you can commute a part of the accumulated corpus and withdraw it in cash for your financial needs. The remaining corpus can then be used to receive annuities.
Now that you know the different types of life insurance plans, here’s a comparative table for quick reference –
How to choose the right type of life insurance policy in India?
Choosing the right type of life insurance policy is essential for securing your financial future. The policy you choose should align with your personal goals, financial objectives, and the level of coverage you need. Consider your family’s financial needs, your health, and whether you need life cover, savings, or both. A little research can go a long way in ensuring you make the right decision.
Here are some factors that can help you find the right types of life insurance policies for your needs –
Life Goals:When selecting a life insurance policy, it’s crucial to consider your life goals. If you're looking for long-term financial protection, choose a policy that offers both savings and coverage. If your goal is to secure your family’s financial future, a term or whole life insurance plan may be more appropriate. Tailor your insurance policy to meet your needs.
Sum Assured:The sum assured is the amount your nominee will be paid in the event of your death. Verify that the sum assured is enough to satisfy the everyday needs of your family, like living expenses, schooling, and paying off debts. You should evaluate your own financial obligations to determine an appropriate sum assured for your policy.
Policy Term:The policy term refers to the time period that the life insurance policy will remain in force. Choosing the appropriate policy term is important. A shorter term might save you on premiums, but it may not save your family from a loss of coverage later on. A longer term provides a method for your family to be covered over a longer period, in addition to securing peace of mind.
Riders:Riders are optional extensions to your life insurance available at an additional nominal premium. They offer expanded coverage for accidental death, critical illness, or disability. Riders may enhance your policy and ultimately offer further protection, depending on your needs. They can also be the most cost-efficient means to expand your coverage without needing to buy a different policy.
Credibility of the Insurer:Prior to acquiring life insurance, you should check the credibility of the life insurer. Make sure to select a provider you can strongly endorse, which means selecting a life insurer that has a strong reputation, solid customer reviews, and a high claim settlement ratio. You also want to ensure the life insurer has a pattern of paying on time and that their policy terms and conditions are clear and transparent.
How to purchase a life insurance policy in India?
After you choose the type of life insurance policy that fits your needs, the next step is to buy it. You can choose one or more types of life insurance plans based on your needs. To buy, there are two options –
Offline
You can buy any type of life insurance policy offline through the branch office of the insurance company or through its intermediary. To buy offline, here’s what you can do –
Assess and compare the types of life insurance policies available and choose the plans that match your needs.
Understand the terms and conditions of the plan and the premium payable
Fill out the offline proposal form and attach the relevant documents
Submit the proposal form along with the premium
Online
To buy online, you can visit the website of the insurance company or download its mobile app and explore the types of life insurance plans available. Then, here’s what you can do –
Fill out the online proposal form and submit it
Upload the relevant documents
Underwriting will review the proposal if approved, pay the premium online through any digital channel.
Tax Benefits on Types of Life InsuranceLife insurance policies offer tax benefits under various sections of the Income Tax Act,1961. Premiums paid are eligible for tax deductions, and in some cases, the maturity proceeds are tax-free.
Section 80D2:Under Section 80D, you can claim tax deductions for premiums paid on life insurance policies with health-related riders. The tax benefit is available for premiums paid for self, spouse, children, and parents. The maximum deduction allowed is ₹25,000 (₹50,000 for senior citizens) if you opt for the old tax regime. This section helps reduce your taxable income, making it easier to save on taxes while securing your family’s future. It’s important to keep your receipts and documents for tax filing purposes.
Section 80C1:Under Section 80C, life insurance premiums are eligible for tax deductions up to ₹1.5 lakh per year. This section also covers other tax-saving instruments like Public Provident Fund (PPF) and National Savings Certificates (NSC). It’s one of the most common ways to save on taxes while ensuring your family’s financial protection. Deductions under 80C only apply to the old tax regime.
Section 10(10D)1:According to Section 10(10D) of the Income Tax Act, the maturity proceeds from life insurance policies are generally tax-free, subject to the satisfaction of conditions. This includes the death benefit and the maturity amount paid to the nominee. However, there are certain conditions where this benefit does not apply, such as if the premiums paid exceed 10% of the sum assured. It's essential to read the policy terms to understand the tax benefits and conditions.
Key takeaways
There are different types of life insurance plans in India, and each plan has its own set of features and benefits.
The popular types of life insurance plans include term insurance plans, endowment plans, ULIPs, money back plans, annuity plans, group plans, etc.
You should choose a suitable life insurance policy based on your financial goals and needs.
You can buy any type of life insurance policy online or offline.
You can enjoy tax benefits on your life insurance policies too under Sections 80C, 80D and 10(10D).
Conclusion
Making an informed decision is important when purchasing a life insurance plan. The sections above explain different types of life insurance and some of the benefits of each one of them. Before you choose a plan, you need to analyse your preferences and your family's requirements. Figure out the financial coverage you want for your family, your premium budget, and the benefits offered by a specific life insurance plan. This way, you can land on a plan that suits your requirements the most.
FAQs
What are the 4 major types of life insurance?The four major types of life insurance plans are term plans, endowment plans, ULIPs, and annuity plans.
What benefits do different types of life insurance policies offer?
Life insurance policies offer different types of benefits. Some of the benefits of life insurance are as follows –
Insurance coverage
Creating savings for your financial goals
Peace of mind
Tax benefits.
Can I purchase two different types of life policies at the same time?
Yes, you can have multiple insurance policies for life insurance. Many people prefer buying term insurance with endowment or ULIP policies together.
What is the ideal age to get life insurance?
It’s never too late to get life insurance. It is ideal to get term insurance earlier in life when there are lower premiums. This ensures a good premium rate as well as financial security in later years.
What is the ideal life insurance for a married couple?
The best type of life insurance for a married couple could be joint life insurance. Joint life insurance policies insure both policyholders under one policy, which financially covers the family. It also depends upon the financial goals of the couple.
What is the ideal life insurance for single individuals?
Term insurance may be considered one of the best option. With term insurance, you can afford the coverage required to protect family members or dependents financially in case of any unforeseen event like death.
Are riders a type of life insurance policy?
No, riders are not a type of life insurance policy. They are additional coverage options that the insured can add to a life insurance policy on the payment of additional nominal premium for extra protection. Examples could include critical illness benefit riders or accidental death benefit riders.
Which type of life insurance policy never expires?
Whole life insurance is often known as permanent life insurance. While term insurance expires after a certain time, whole life insurance lasts a lifetime.
What are some examples of life insurance plans?
Some types of life insurance plans are term insurance, whole life insurance, endowment insurance, and unit-linked insurance plans (ULIPs).
Is whole life insurance more expensive than a term plan?
Yes, whole life insurance typically has higher premiums due to the lifelong coverage it provides.
Is it complicated to calculate the premium for term insurance online?
No, calculating the premium for term insurance online is simple. Many insurers offer easy-to-use premium calculators on their websites.

ULIPs are different from the traditional insurance products and are subject to the risk factors. The premium paid in ULIPs are subject to investment risks associated with capital markets and the NAVs of the units may go up or down based on the performance of fund and factors influencing the capital market and the insured is responsible for his/her decisions. Types of life insurance
Life insurance plans help in financial planning by creating a secure corpus on the unfortunate death of the life assured during the policy term. With a life insurance policy, you can get financial security for yourself and your family.
Moreover, there are different types of life insurance plans with their own set of benefits and relevance. You can choose from these policies based on your financial goals, investment horizon and needs. So, let’s assess the different types of life insurance plans available in the market and what each plan means.
8 Popular Types of Life Insurance Plans
As mentioned earlier, there are different types of life insurance plans available in the market. Let’s look at the eight most popular types of plans –
1. Term Insurance Plans:
As the name suggests, term insurance is purchased for a specific tenure. If the insured dies
during the plan's tenure, the nominee of the insured receives a death benefit.
Check out the various features of the plan:
a. A term plan is purchased for a fixed tenure.
b. Term insurance is a life cover offering death benefits if the insured dies during the
tenure.
c. There are various types of term insurance plans, such as level cover, increasing
cover, decreasing cover and term plan with a return of premium.
d. The plan may give you a choice to add riders on payment of an additional nominal
premium, such as:
i. Accidental Permanent Total and Partial Disability Rider
ii. Waiver of Premium Rider
iii. Accidental Death Benefit Rider
iv. Critical Illness Benefit Rider, among others.
e. A term insurance calculator helps you calculate the coverage you may need for your
family in your absence.
f. Pure term insurance plans do not offer any maturity benefit. TROP (Term Plan with
Return of Premium) has a maturity benefit wherein the premium paid for the plan is
returned to the policyholder if he survives the policy tenure, subject to certain
deductions.
g. Tax Benefit:
i. The premium paid towards a term insurance plan is deductible under
Section 80C (under the Old Tax Regime) of the Income Tax Act, 1961, up to
Rs 1.5 lakhs per annum, subject to the provisions stated therein.
ii. Maturity benefit, in the case of term plans with return of premium, is also
tax-free under section 10(10D), subject to satisfaction of conditions
mentioned therein.
iii. As per section 10(10D) of the Income Tax Act, 1961, the death benefits
received by your nominee under the plan are tax-free.
2. ULIPs – Unit Linked Insurance Plans:
ULIP stands for Unit Linked Insurance Plans. If you are looking for a comprehensive
insurance plan that offers both life cover and a market-linked investment component, ULIP
can be a suitable choice. Through ULIPs, the premium you pay is invested in market-linked
funds, post deduction of applicable charges. Along with market-linked returns, ULIPs also
provide a life cover to the life assured. It is essential to note that ULIP is a type of life
insurance suitable for those who want a long-term investment plan, as it comes with a 5-
year lock-in period.
Let's have a look at some of the features of ULIPs:
○ ULIP provides both a life cover component and a market-linked investment
component. The policyholder can choose from various market-linked funds as
per their requirements.
○ Money can be invested in different funds like equity funds, debt funds, hybrid
funds or balanced funds as per your risk appetite. Over time, you may switch
your funds under ULIP depending on the terms and conditions of the plan.
○ You can use the ULIP calculator that uses data provided by you to give an idea of
the estimated returns. The expected rate of return, premium amount and
duration can be modified to get different results as required.
○ Tax Benefit:
○ ULIPs offer three types of tax benefits:
■ The premium is deductible under section 80C of the Income Tax Act,
1961(under the old tax regime), up to Rs 1.5 lakhs a year
■ For ULIPs issued on or after February 1, 2021, the maturity amount
remains tax-free if the aggregate annual premium in any financial year
during tenure of the policy/policies does not exceed Rs 2.5 lakh and
subject to satisfaction of Section 10(10D) conditions.
■ For ULIPs issued before February 01, 2021, the maturity benefits are
exempt from tax, subject to the satisfaction of conditions mentioned in
Section 10(10D) of the Income Tax Act, 1961
■ However, for ULIPs issued on or after February 01, 2021, with an annual
premium of more than Rs. 2.50 lakhs, gains will be taxable as Capital
gains. The LTCG will be tax-free up to Rs. .1.25 lakh for the given financial
year for all ULIPs.
■ Moreover, LTCG gains beyond the exempted limits will be taxable @
12.5% for all types of ULIPs, whether equity or debt-oriented.
■ The death benefit is tax-exempt under section 10(10D) of the Income Tax
Act 1961, irrespective of the date of issuance of the ULIP policy.
■ ULIPs give you the option to switch the funds you invest in during the
policy term, subject to policy terms & conditions. These switches are not
taxable.
3. Endowment Insurance Plans:
Some people want a life cover that can also offer a savings option. Under an endowment
plan, you get both of these options. However, if the insured passes away during the tenure,
the nominee receives the sum assured and the savings components & the periodic bonuses,
if any, depending on the Insurer and policy terms and conditions. This way, an endowment
plan ensures both life cover and savings benefits. Take a quick look at the various features of
an endowment plan:
○ The premium paid for the endowment plan is used to provide life insurance
cover to the insured and also offer a savings component.
○ Tax Benefits:
■ The maturity benefit is tax-free under section 10(10D) of the Income Tax
Act, 1961, subject to the satisfaction of conditions mentioned therein.
The policyholder can claim deductions on the premiums paid under Section 80C of the
Income Tax Act, 1961, up to Rs 1.5 lakhs a year, as per the provisions stated therein under
the Old Tax Regime.
4. Whole Life Insurance Plans:
A whole life insurance plan, as the name suggests, is an insurance plan that provides cover
for your whole life, i.e up to the age of 99 or 100 years, depending upon the insurer. This is a
type of life insurance plan that has both survival benefits and death benefits. A whole life
insurance plan provides a death benefit to your nominees after you are no longer around.
Here is a quick look at some of the features of the whole life insurance plan:
○ A whole life insurance plan provides coverage to the life assured until he/she
turn 99 or 100 years old.
○ Upon the insured's death during the plan's tenure, the nominee receives a death
benefit.
○ The plan may also pay out a survival benefit if the life assured survives the policy
term, depending on the terms and conditions of the policy.
○ Tax Benefits:
■ The premiums paid under the policy are deductible up to Rs 1.5 lakh
under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961, subject to provisions
stated therein under the Old Tax Regime.
■ The death benefit as well as the maturity benefit of a whole life
insurance plan are exempted from tax under Section 10 (10D) of the
Income Tax Act, 1961, subject to satisfaction of the provisions stated
therein.
5. Child Insurance Plans:
Once a child is born, parents experience a lot of responsibilities. To fulfil a number of these,
parents may require adequate finances. Some new parents may start investing for their
children as soon as they are born. Child insurance plans are one such investment. These
plans provide a combination of protection and wealth creation to ensure a secure financial
future for your child. A child insurance plan helps build a steady corpus that can be used to
finance your child’s life goals. The life cover that comes along with it offers a death benefit
to your nominee(s) if something happens to you.
A child insurance plan provides payouts at different milestones of your child’s life. It may be
for their higher education, wedding, etc.
Some of the features of the plan are:
○ A child insurance plan provides a maturity benefit to the child and a life insurance
cover to the insured parent
○ Some child insurance plans have a waiver of premium, wherein if the insured parent
dies within the policy tenure, the future premiums are waived and the plan
continues as per the original schedule.
○ The child/nominee receives a death benefit if the insured passes away during the
tenure. However, the plan continues to pay the survival/maturity benefit of the plan
as per the plan’s terms & conditions.
6. Group Insurance Plans:
As the name suggests, a group insurance plan is meant for a group of people. Under a single
life insurance policy, a group of people are covered. Under an employer-employee group life
insurance scheme, an organisation purchases a group life insurance plan to provide its
employees’ life insurance coverage. In the case of a company, the employer takes out a

employer pays the premium for the same. However, in some cases, a part of the premium is
shared between the employee and the employer as well. An employee is covered under a
group life policy only while they are employed with the company.
Some of the features of Group Insurance Plans under the Employer-Employee Scheme:
○ A group life insurance covers a whole group under a single life insurance plan.
○ The employer is the master policyholder and typically pays the premium on behalf of
its employees
○ Providing group life insurance can give a sense of financial security to the employees
○ Tax Benefits:
■ If an employee contributes towards the premium for the plan, they can
claim the premiums paid as deductions under Section 80C of the Income Tax
Act, 1961 (under the Old Tax Regime)
■ The death benefit payout is also completely exempt from taxation under
section 10(10D) of the Income Tax Act, 1961. subject to the provisions
mentioned therein.
7. Money Back Insurance Plans:
Money Back Insurance Plans provide life insurance with savings. You can get life coverage
while getting periodic payments during the policy period. It is a suitable product for
someone wanting insurance protection and a guaranteed* income. Here's a detailed
breakdown of their features and benefits:
○ These plans offer regular payouts throughout the policy term. These payments are a
fixed percentage of the sum assured and help meet big expenses (like children's
education, the cost of maintaining a home, and medical expenses, etc).
○ If the life assured dies during the policy term, the nominee (or beneficiaries) receives
the death benefit.
○ If the life assured survives the policy term, they receive the sum assured plus any
bonuses accrued during the policy duration. This creates savings for the policyholder
upon the policy's maturity.
○ Money-back insurance Plans are available for different tenures, such as 10 years, 15
years, or 20 years, so you can find the plan that is right for you and your needs.
There is flexibility with respect to policy duration and the option for a joint policy or
rider.
○ Tax Benefits:
■ Premiums paid for Money Back Insurance Plans are eligible for tax
deductions under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act (under the Old tax
regime). You can claim a deduction of up to ₹1.5 lakh annually on
premiums if you opt for the old tax regime.1
■ The sum assured received at maturity, including bonuses, is tax-free
under Section 10(10D), provided the premiums do not exceed 10% of the
sum assured.1

Annuity plans are retirement-oriented life insurance plans that help you secure your golden
years financially. There are two types of annuity plans, deferred and immediate annuity, and
you can choose a plan that matches your needs.
Some of the salient features of annuity plans are as follows –
a. Deferred annuity plans are savings-oriented plans that provides a regular income
starting at a future date chosen by the policyholder and allow you to create a retirement corpus over your active working life. b. Deferred annuity plans can be offered as traditional endowment plans or ULIPs
c. Immediate annuity plans provides regular income starting immediately after you pay
a lump sum (purchase price) and meant to create steady inflow of annuity in your golden years. d. You have to pay a single premium, called the purchase price, to buy immediate
annuity plans. e. Under deferred annuity plans, you can commute a part of the accumulated corpus
and withdraw it in cash for your financial needs. The remaining corpus can then be used to receive annuities.
Now that you know the different types of life insurance plans, here’s a comparative table for quick reference –
Types of life insurance plans
Brief description Benefits paid Purpose
Term insurance plans
Protection-oriented life insurance plans that aim to provide financial assistance to your family in your absence
Death benefit is paid in pure term insurance. Premiums are refunded on maturity under return- of-premium term plans
Income replacement or financial security
ULIPs Insurance-cum-investment plans that help you earn market-linked returns
Death benefit or maturity benefit
Market-linked returns
Endowment plans
Savings-oriented life insurance plans that help you create a stable corpus for your financial goals
Death benefit or maturity benefit
Savings for financial goals
Whole life plans Savings-oriented life insurance plans that run lifelong usually upto 99 or 100 years
Death benefit or maturity benefit
Whole life protection with savings Child insurance plans
Savings-oriented life insurance plans that are designed to create a secure corpus for the child, even if the parent passes away
Death benefit or maturity benefit
Creating a secure corpus for the child’s needs
Group insurance plans
Life insurance plans are meant to cover a group of individuals under the same policy
Death benefit Employee welfare
Money-back plans
Savings-oriented life insurance plans that offer liquidity through periodic money-back benefits
Money back benefits, death benefit or maturity benefit
Savings for financial goals with added liquidity Annuity plans Retirement-oriented life insurance plans that help in creating a retirement corpus or a steady flow of annuity
Deferred annuity plans - Death benefit or maturity benefit (before vesting) Immediate annuity plans – annuity payouts (with optional death benefit, based on the annuity option chosen)
Retirement planning
How to choose the right type of life insurance policy in India?
Choosing the right type of life insurance policy is essential for securing your financial future. The policy you choose should align with your personal goals, financial objectives, and the level of coverage you need. Consider your family’s financial needs, your health, and whether you need life cover, savings, or both. A little research can go a long way in ensuring you make the right decision.
Here are some factors that can help you find the right types of life insurance policies for your needs –
● Life Goals: When selecting a life insurance policy, it’s crucial to consider your life goals. If you're looking for long-term financial protection, choose a policy that offers both savings and coverage. If your goal is to secure your family’s financial future, a term or whole life insurance plan may be more appropriate. Tailor your insurance policy to meet your needs. ● Sum Assured: The sum assured is the amount your nominee will be paid in the event of your death. Verify that the sum assured is enough to satisfy the everyday needs of your family, like living expenses, schooling, and paying off debts. You should evaluate your own financial obligations to determine an appropriate sum assured for your policy. ● Policy Term: The policy term refers to the time period that the life insurance policy will remain in force. Choosing the appropriate policy term is important. A shorter term might save you on premiums, but it may not save your family from a loss of coverage later on. A longer term provides a method for your family to be covered over a longer period, in addition to securing peace of mind. ● Riders: Riders are optional extensions to your life insurance available at an additional nominal premium. They offer expanded coverage for accidental death, critical illness, or disability. Riders may enhance your policy and ultimately offer further protection, depending on your needs. They can also be the most cost-efficient means to expand your coverage without needing to buy a different policy. ● Credibility of the Insurer: Prior to acquiring life insurance, you should check the credibility of the life insurer. Make sure to select a provider you can strongly endorse, which means selecting a life insurer that has a strong reputation, solid customer reviews, and a high claim settlement ratio. You also want to ensure the life insurer has a pattern of paying on time and that their policy terms and conditions are clear and transparent.
How to purchase a life insurance policy in India?
After you choose the type of life insurance policy that fits your needs, the next step is to buy it. You can choose one or more types of life insurance plans based on your needs. To buy, there are two options –
● Offline You can buy any type of life insurance policy offline through the branch office of the insurance company or through its intermediary. To buy offline, here’s what you can do –
o Assess and compare the types of life insurance policies available and choose the
plans that match your needs. o Understand the terms and conditions of the plan and the premium payable o Fill out the offline proposal form and attach the relevant documents o Submit the proposal form, underwriting will review the same if approved pay the
premium ● Online To buy online, you can visit the website of the insurance company or download its mobile app and explore the types of life insurance plans available. Then, here’s what you can do –
o Fill out the online proposal form and submit it o Upload the relevant documents o Underwriting will review the proposal, if approved, pay the premium online through
any digital channel.
Tax Benefits on Types of Life Insurance Life insurance policies offer tax benefits under various sections of the Income Tax Act,1961. Premiums paid are eligible for tax deductions, and in some cases, the maturity proceeds are tax-free.
● Section 80D2:
Under Section 80D, you can claim tax deductions for premiums paid on life insurance policies with health-related riders. The tax benefit is available for premiums paid for self, spouse, children, and parents. The maximum deduction allowed is ₹25,000 (₹50,000 for senior citizens) if you opt for the old tax regime. This section helps reduce your taxable income, making it easier to save on taxes while securing your family’s future. It’s important to keep your receipts and documents for tax filing purposes. ● Section 80C1:
Under Section 80C, life insurance premiums are eligible for tax deductions up to ₹1.5 lakh per year. This section also covers other tax-saving instruments like Public Provident Fund (PPF) and National Savings Certificates (NSC). It’s one of the most common ways to save on taxes while ensuring your family’s financial protection. Deductions under 80C only apply to the old tax regime. ● Section 10(10D)1:
According to Section 10(10D) of the Income Tax Act, the maturity proceeds from life insurance policies are generally tax-free, subject to the satisfaction of conditions. This includes the death benefit and the maturity amount paid to the nominee. However, there are certain conditions where this benefit does not apply, such as if the premiums paid exceed 10% of the sum assured. It's essential to read the policy terms to understand the tax benefits and conditions.
Key takeaways
● There are different types of life insurance plans in India, and each plan has its own set of features and benefits. ● The popular types of life insurance plans include term insurance plans, endowment plans, ULIPs, money back plans, annuity plans, group plans, etc. ● You should choose a suitable life insurance policy based on your financial goals and needs.
● You can buy any type of life insurance policy online or offline. ● You can enjoy tax benefits on your life insurance policies too under Sections 80C, 80D and 10(10D).
Conclusion
Making an informed decision is important when purchasing a life insurance plan. The sections above explain different types of life insurance and some of the benefits of each one of them. Before you choose a plan, you need to analyse your preferences and your family's requirements. Figure out the financial coverage you want for your family, your premium budget, and the benefits offered by a specific life insurance plan. This way, you can land on a plan that suits your requirements the most.
FAQs
1. What are the 4 major types of life insurance?
The four major types of life insurance plans are term plans, endowment plans, ULIPs, and annuity plans. 2. What benefits do different types of life insurance policies offer?
Life insurance policies offer different types of benefits. Some of the benefits of life insurance are as follows –
a. Insurance coverage b. Creating savings for your financial goals c. Peace of mind d. Tax benefits.
3. Can I purchase two different types of life policies at the same time?
Yes, you can have multiple insurance policies for life insurance. Many people prefer buying term insurance with endowment or ULIP policies together.
4. What is the ideal age to get life insurance?
It’s never too late to get life insurance. It is ideal to get term insurance earlier in life when there are lower premiums. This ensures a good premium rate as well as financial security in later years.
5. What is the ideal life insurance for a married couple?
The best type of life insurance for a married couple could be joint life insurance. Joint life insurance policies insure both policyholders under one policy, which financially covers the family. It also depends upon the financial goals of the couple.
6. What is the ideal life insurance for single individuals?
Term insurance may be considered one of the best option. With term insurance, you can afford the coverage required to protect family members or dependents financially in case of any unforeseen event like death.
7. Are riders a type of life insurance policy?
No, riders are not a type of life insurance policy. They are additional coverage options that the insured can add to a life insurance policy on the payment of additional nominal premium
for extra protection. Examples could include critical illness benefit riders or accidental death benefit riders.
8. Which type of life insurance policy never expires? Whole life insurance is often known as permanent life insurance. While term insurance expires after a certain time, whole life insurance lasts a lifetime.
9. What are some examples of life insurance plans?
Some types of life insurance plans are term insurance, whole life insurance, endowment insurance, and unit-linked insurance plans (ULIPs).
10. Is whole life insurance more expensive than a term plan?
Yes, whole life insurance typically has higher premiums due to the lifelong coverage it provides.
11. Is it complicated to calculate the premium for term insurance online?
No, calculating the premium for term insurance online is simple. Many insurers offer easy- to-use premium calculators on their websites.

○ ULIPs offer three types of tax benefits:
■ The premium is deductible under section 80C of the Income Tax Act,
1961(under the old tax regime), up to Rs 1.5 lakhs a year
■ For ULIPs issued on or after February 1, 2021, the maturity amount
remains tax-free if the aggregate annual premium in any financial year
during tenure of the policy/policies does not exceed Rs 2.5 lakh and
subject to satisfaction of Section 10(10D) conditions.
■ For ULIPs issued before February 01, 2021, the maturity benefits are
exempt from tax, subject to the satisfaction of conditions mentioned in
Section 10(10D) of the Income Tax Act, 1961
■ However, for ULIPs issued on or after February 01, 2021, with an annual
premium of more than Rs. 2.50 lakhs, gains will be taxable as Capital
gains.
The LTCG will be tax-free up to Rs. .1.25 lakh for the given financial
year for all ULIPs.
■ Moreover, LTCG gains beyond the exempted limits will be taxable @
12.5% for all types of ULIPs, whether equity or debt-oriented.
■ The death benefit is tax-exempt under section 10(10D) of the Income Tax
Act 1961, irrespective of the date of issuance of the ULIP policy.
■ ULIPs give you the option to switch the funds you invest in during the
policy term, subject to policy terms & conditions.

changes.word_original: There are two types of annuity plans, deferred and immediate annuity, and you can choose a plan that matches your needs.
changes.pdf_final: ○ A group life insurance covers a whole group under a single life insurance plan.
○ The employer is the master policyholder and typically pays the premium on behalf of
its employees
○ Providing group life insurance can give a sense of financial security to the employees
○ Tax Benefits:
■ If an employee contributes towards the premium for the plan, they can
claim the premiums paid as deductions under Section 80C of the Income Tax
Act, 1961 (under the Old Tax Regime)
■ The death benefit payout is also completely exempt from taxation under
section 10(10D) of the Income Tax Act, 1961. subject to the provisions
mentioned therein.
Money Back Insurance Plans:
Money Back Insurance Plans provide life insurance with savings.
You can get life coverage
while getting periodic payments during the policy period.
It is a suitable product for
someone wanting insurance protection and a guaranteed* income.
Here's a detailed
breakdown of their features and benefits:
○ These plans offer regular payouts throughout the policy term.
These payments are a
fixed percentage of the sum assured and help meet big expenses (like children's
education, the cost of maintaining a home, and medical expenses, etc).
○ If the life assured dies during the policy term, the nominee (or beneficiaries) receives
the death benefit.
○ If the life assured survives the policy term, they receive the sum assured plus any
bonuses accrued during the policy duration.
This creates savings for the policyholder
upon the policy's maturity.
○ Money-back insurance Plans are available for different tenures, such as 10 years, 15
years, or 20 years, so you can find the plan that is right for you and your needs.
There is flexibility with respect to policy duration and the option for a joint policy or
rider.
○ Tax Benefits:
■ Premiums paid for Money Back Insurance Plans are eligible for tax
deductions under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act (under the Old tax
regime).
You can claim a deduction of up to ₹1.5 lakh annually on
premiums if you opt for the old tax regime.1
■ The sum assured received at maturity, including bonuses, is tax-free
under Section 10(10D), provided the premiums do not exceed 10% of the
sum assured.1
Annuity plans:
Annuity plans are retirement-oriented life insurance plans that help you secure your golden
years financially.
There are two types of annuity plans, deferred and immediate annuity, and
you can choose a plan that matches your needs.

changes.word_original: Deferred annuity plans are savings-oriented plans that allow you to create a retirement corpus over your active working life.
changes.pdf_final: a.
Deferred annuity plans are savings-oriented plans that provides a regular income
starting at a future date chosen by the policyholder and allow you to create a retirement corpus over your active working life. b.

changes.word_original: Immediate annuity plans are meant to create a regular and steady inflow of annuity in your golden years.
changes.pdf_final: c.

changes.word_original: You have to pay a single premium, called the purchase price, to buy immediate annuity plans.
changes.pdf_final: Immediate annuity plans provides regular income starting immediately after you pay

changes.word_original: Under deferred annuity plans, you can commute a part of the accumulated corpus and withdraw it in cash for your financial needs.
changes.pdf_final: a lump sum (purchase price) and meant to create steady inflow of annuity in your golden years. d.
You have to pay a single premium, called the purchase price, to buy immediate
annuity plans. e.
Under deferred annuity plans, you can commute a part of the accumulated corpus
and withdraw it in cash for your financial needs.
Types of life insurance plans
Brief description Benefits paid Purpose
Term insurance plans
Protection-oriented life insurance plans that aim to provide financial assistance to your family in your absence
Death benefit is paid in pure term insurance.
Premiums are refunded on maturity under return- of-premium term plans
Income replacement or financial security
ULIPs Insurance-cum-investment plans that help you earn market-linked returns
Death benefit or maturity benefit
Market-linked returns
Endowment plans
Savings-oriented life insurance plans that help you create a stable corpus for your financial goals
Death benefit or maturity benefit
Savings for financial goals
Whole life plans Savings-oriented life insurance plans that run lifelong usually upto 99 or 100 years
Death benefit or maturity benefit
Whole life protection with savings Child insurance plans
Savings-oriented life insurance plans that are designed to create a secure corpus for the child, even if the parent passes away
Death benefit or maturity benefit
Creating a secure corpus for the child’s needs
Group insurance plans
Life insurance plans are meant to cover a group of individuals under the same policy
Death benefit Employee welfare
Money-back plans
Savings-oriented life insurance plans that offer liquidity through periodic money-back benefits
Money back benefits, death benefit or maturity benefit
Savings for financial goals with added liquidity Annuity plans Retirement-oriented life insurance plans that help in creating a retirement corpus or a steady flow of annuity
Deferred annuity plans - Death benefit or maturity benefit (before vesting) Immediate annuity plans – annuity payouts (with optional death benefit, based on the annuity option chosen)
Retirement planning

changes.word_original: There are different types of life insurance plans in India, and each plan has its own set of features and benefits.
